video_title: Why your old retirement plan may fail
transcript: Welcome to new episode of Power Players with Brian Sazi. I am our host Brian Sazi. It's me in the flesh and I want to talk about all things retirement today and annuities. You know, you may not think it's fun but my guest here sure does think it's fun. Mike Dowdall, co-president Athene USA and CEO of Athene Holding. Good to see you. >> Great to see you again, Brian. >> Um I always ask you this. I'll ask you again. What's new in annuities? >> There's lots new in annuities and the reason there's lots new in annuities people keep getting older. >> Yeah. >> Right? And so we've had, you know, every day there's 12,000 people, new people turning age 65. Every day. That's a lot of new retirees. All that need more assets. >> Um I went through ahead of this and I haven't done this before. I was just curious what people are searching for right now in terms of retirement and the one that came out on top everybody thinks the magic number for retirement is 1.46 million dollars. Is that enough? Is that enough? >> It's all about planning, right? So for many that can be enough and one of the things that I I get excited about uh which you asked in addition to skiing and other activities is um what you can do to your portfolio, that 1.46 million if you integrate annuities inside that strategy. Right? So most people they probably come up with that magic number because they say, well, I can withdraw about 4% every year. 4% of 1 and 1/2 million that's about 60,000 a year. >> do the math. I just picked the number. It was like 1.46 >> And and then so you take um you know, with annuities you can basically take out 1 and 1/2 times as much. So take out 6% versus 4%. So you can basically change your retirement income from 60 to 90,000 dollars. And that's the power that annuities kind of bring to the table. >> 1.46 million doesn't sound like enough to me. Everything is prices of everything are going up in the world but people are living longer. So maybe that is not the magic number. >> Yeah, and again, it depends how you manage it, right? So, yeah, the biggest risk that a lot of retirees face is you know, they're generally one downturn away from potentially running out of money, right? So, 1.46, 1 and 1/2 million feels like potentially enough. But, if there is a you know, market downturn, a 10-20% correction, that could be a 10-20% hit to the portfolio because a lot of a lot of people that are close to retirement still have a lot of money in equities. They might even in their 50s and 60s still have about 70% in equities. And again, that's where, you know, we get excited about annuities because when you fold in annuities, you can create that guaranteed lifetime income. >> Is it hard to get someone excited about annuities when the stock market is hitting new records at the same time there's a war? Like, this doesn't make any sense. >> Yeah, it is a I mean, it's it's a little hard competing against the bull market, but the sales have been at all-time highs and that's it's largely because of the the interest rate the the you know, the interest rates were near zero pre pre COVID and they've really risen to more long-term natural levels. And what that's done is it's really closed the gap between what you can get in the stock market in a normal course, 8 to 10% and then what you can get on a guaranteed basis with annuities, which is upwards of about five 5%. Um and so, it closes the gap. And the other thing that annuities offer is tax deferral. Uh and so, imagine getting that guaranteed return on your assets plus tax deferral with zero market exposure. >> Let's say I have a large part of my savings in a CD. Uh and I should know this because I do. That's me. >> Mhm. >> Is would my investments be Is that How safe is that? If I took it out of a CD and put it into annuity, like what return should I expect? >> Yeah. Hey, it's incredibly safe because in the insurance world, all annuities are backed by capital, so which is effectively a rainy day fund to protect the policy holder. And in terms of what you can expect, you can generally expect about two 2% more every year. If you take a high-rated insurance carrier and compare it to a high-rated bank and look at just the deltas and and the expected yield, it's it's a average is around 2%. So imagine starting earlier. A lot of people start buying annuities at around age 60. Imagine starting in your mid-40s and just keep rolling it over for 20 years. That's 20 years of an additional 2% every year. And if it's post-tax money >> It really adds up. >> It adds up a lot. And so it's just taking easy lazy money and making it work a lot harder and smarter. And that's that's a power of annuities is you can take the same amount of savings and if you get in early, you can accumulate with respect to the safe assets like CDs and money markets just a lot more over time. >> Like I know CDs, I'm not It's there. It's safe. >> I mean, can I lose my money in annuities? >> No. So the principal is there. >> Principal is 100% protected. And then the simple they're called MYGAs, which is just a guaranteed rate to term annuity. Think of it as like the acronym of a CD, but attached to the annuity space. You have that guaranteed for the full term. And then what customers generally do is they'll buy a 5-year commitment and at the end of the commitment, they'll renew into a new 5-year MYGA. Again, akin to a CD, but doing it all under a tax-deferred wrapper and doing it all with getting higher yield every year. So guaranteed principal, guaranteed excess return. >> Why do you think sales have been at a record this year? >> I'd say I think it's it's a couple of things. One is the the interest rate environment has really helped increase the yield that we can offer. When rates were at an all-time row low, you could you know, the similar annuity might have been offering only about 3% potentially even under 3%. So that extra yield has really created a lot of demand for people to get into them get into the annuity space and I think just the general aging of the population and growing awareness of annuities and what they can do to your portfolio. >> Yeah, like I mentioned at the top, people are living longer a lot of cases like significantly longer. How how does that change annuity industry longer term? >> So long longer term we think it's a catalyst for growth, right? So it's it's you have the backdrop of the again the 12,000 new age 65 retirees every day and then look against that the $4 trillion retirement gap. And one of the things that I think it's it's hard to ask middle class savers to save more. Their their you know their their pocketbooks are effectively allocated to daily living expenses and then you have some for retirement. It's hard to squeeze out more on that retirement savings. And so the the power of of annuities is again starting early you can start to close that savings gap because you can make that portfolio feel a lot richer. So again going back to your your one and a half million dollars, I used the example of taking 6% every year out versus 4%. The other way to think about it is it's it's powerful as having a portfolio that's well over $2 million in terms of you know the the income power that annuities can provide. So it just makes your savings feel that much stronger. >> Are you seeing you within you know your sales data, do you see under 30 year olds getting into annuities? >> No, not a lot of that. And I think a lot of it's just stigma of historically annuities have kind of been thought of as just tools for retirees as opposed to savings tools that even younger people can get into. And so there's a couple misunderstood conceptions. So one is annuities aren't for retirees only. They can really make a lot more sense at a minimum in your 40s and there's some types of products that do make sense in your 30s. So, there's a um a a product that it's the the technical term is a RILA, but it's a it's a a product that provides a lot of upside, some downside protection, but it functions more like a like an equity, but with guardrails. Uh so, think of being in the in the equity market, but instead of having unprotected downside, you have some protected downside and and some of these types of solutions skew younger. Uh and I think as a as an industry, we just need to do a better job at reaching out to these individuals and connecting them >> There was a story in the Wall Street Journal like 2 weeks ago on how younger folks are just have been conditioned to buy the dip in the stock market. And it was they they profiled one person in there that have just they they put their life savings into the stock into a stock um that they didn't know much about, but they did anyway. And you have to pull your hair out when you see stories like this um because they're not protected. >> Correct. They're not They're not protected. And who can play on the dip? >> Yeah. >> I don't I wish I knew where the dip was. I'd I'd be >> I would be a lot wealthier. We wouldn't be sitting here. >> That's right. >> What you know, I look at the markets and there's been I mean, there's always been formal innovations. I look at prediction markets. That's one of the latest innovations, I think. Like, what is has there been innovations in the annuity space? >> There have and and I think there's a lot to come. So, so one of the more recent innovations has actually been in the defined contribution area. >> Mhm. >> So, defined contribution historically has been you know, if you go back 30 years 30 years ago, the default investment would be like a stable value fund, which is a very low returning, very safe um or a balanced fund, which would have been like 50% bonds, 50% equities. And the dominant uh solution today is morphed into target date funds, which is effectively uh a glide path, which is automatically de-risks your savings portfolio as you get older. Um so, it might be 80% equities or even 100% in your 20s and by the time you get to close to 65 it might be 50/50. One of the biggest innovations in that space is is one that Athena has brought to the table is integrating annuities directly inside a target date fund and effectively what you have is a better target date fund. Uh and and what we think just as target date funds have become effectively the default in the industry because it's just a better mouse trap for savers. It takes the it takes the it makes savings a lot easier because instead of having to manage your own what what what's my allocation should be it just automatically gets reallocated for you. The what what uh our our product does is it seamlessly integrates an annuity inside a target date fund. So you now have instead of just a pile of money that accumulates at retirement you have a built-in guaranteed lifetime income when you get out the other side. One of the biggest innovations and I think another one that will be coming is is uh that I'm excited to see is I think how AI is going to streamline how annuities are bought and sold and really simplify the process. >> How is it How are they going to do that? >> Well, so today the way annuities they're very much of a sold not bought product and and a big reason is you have annuities are somewhat of an orphan off to the side an insurance product that sits kind of outside the sphere the retirement ecosystem sphere. So a lot of times in planning software they don't sit inside the planning software. They're just off to the side. And so an advisor has to explain to a customer how it fits in. Um they typically don't show up on you know if you're looking at your accounts on your whatever you know statement system you work with they don't show up on that. Um and so the way tech we see technology emerging is the ability to be able to model annuities directly inside planning software. Well, because it'll be a lot easier to do and just think of eventually pushing a button where you say what's my recommended allocation instead of 60% equity 40% bonds to over simplify it. Hey, if 40% equity is 20% fixed index annuity which is guaranteed protection equity like upside half your bond portfolio put in a you know a simple my guy savings product. And it should be that easy and I think the technology now exists where we'll be able to make that happen as an industry and that should help create better access to products both in terms of the number of people that can get protected as well as really reach to a younger age demographic. >> How is there a certain date the switch flips on this or it's just going to happen over time? >> it's going to happen over time and and it's the road has been marching that way but slowly. And the development of a lot of the AI tools I think is bringing that road map in quickly and there's an opportunity I think for insurance to really be sitting sitting alongside and completely integrated into a retirement plan. These are largely their insurance products but they look and feel like investment and savings products and they need to show up that way. >> I'm willing to assume that this in fact does happen we will see this very soon. How should one go about picking an amazing annuity? Like what makes an amazing annuity? >> Right so it's a number of things one is quality of the carrier. You know so for example you want to look at the rating of a company so A&M Best is a is a very common one used when looking at it from a customer lens or if an advisor is evaluating a company and you generally want A or better Athene is an A plus. Uh size of the balance sheet matters we have about a 400 billion dollar balance sheet within Athene is the number one writer. Amount of capital. Um so the larger the capital stack that means just generally the more assets and the more cushion you have and Athene has about 35 billion dollars of capital. Um and it's also the amount of cushion that exists and there's a metric called risk based capital or RBC ratio and Athene's is over well over four 400% it's approximately above 440% and those are a lot of factors. So quality independent raters, you want a high quality carrier, you want a large company and then it's really about product choice and that's more of a personal selection, right? So the the MYGA products tend to be very good replacements for CDs, money markets and bond funds. Superior performance especially compared to CDs and money markets. With bonds it takes out the rate volatility cuz annuities just march up. And then on the equity side there's a couple there's some options there on in terms of products that will can feel a little bit more like equities. >> How did you get into this field? >> It was so largely so my background is a pension actuary. So I started in in pension work in the in the mid 90s and I watched the decline of retirement defined benefit plans and then over the years I joined the insurance industry in 2008 in the midst of the financial crisis watching just a lot of the pension plans continue to go away the the funding levels go way down and pension plans and by and large traditional defined benefit plans have gone by the wayside. They don't have the portability employees don't stay at the same company for 40 years. And so getting into the annuity business is a way to kind of re-energize retirement and kind of fill a gap that's that's been broken. >> What do you do when you're not playing Mr. Annuity? >> Well when I'm >> You are Mr. Annuity. I've called you that before and I'll call you that again and I don't mean that in a like favorable awesome way. Yeah, it's cool. >> Hey, we're trying to make annuities cool. >> No, I mean like I if I when I need to know anything about retirement annuities I come to you like full stop like >> Hey, it's like one thing I say if you if you think a lot of people think of the annuities that existed 30 years ago if you took a fresh look it's before there were you know 30 years ago there's like one flavor today there's a Swiss Army knife kind of annuities for everybody. When I'm not doing that love to hike. Love >> to ski. >> Good >> to see you, my friend. I appreciate it. All right, and that's it for this episode of Power Players with Brian Urlacher. Appreciate you tuning in.
